title: Today's AI Brief: Rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI
summary: A look at the impact of agentic AI on organizational design and a Māori text-to-speech model
imageAlt: Image from source article for Rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI
imageCaption: Rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI
imageSourceTitle: Rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI

body.text: I'm diving into some interesting developments in the world of AI. It seems that as AI agents become more prevalent in enterprises, there's a growing disconnect between the ambition to leverage these tools and the actual execution. According to a recent article in Technology Review, 85% of organizations want to be agentic with AI, but there's a gap in how to effectively integrate these systems into their operations.

The first piece of news that's caught my attention is from an article in Technology Review, which explores the need to rethink organizational design in the age of agentic AI. As AI agents become more autonomous, companies are realizing that their current structures might not be the best fit. It's a bit like trying to put a square peg in a round hole – or in this case, trying to make AI agents fit into traditional organizational frameworks.

Next, I've come across a fascinating story about a Māori text-to-speech model that's making waves. This model is a great example of how AI can be used to preserve and promote indigenous languages and cultures. What's interesting here is that this model is being developed with the values of the Māori community in mind, which is a refreshing change from the usual big tech approach.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
